([Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"): *statutární město*) is a [municipal corporation](/wiki/Municipal_corporation "Municipal corporation") that has been granted [city status](/wiki/City_status "City status") by [Act of Parliament](/wiki/Act_of_Parliament "Act of Parliament"). It is more prestigious than the simple title **město** ("[town](/wiki/Town "Town")"), which can be awarded by the [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_Czech_Republic "Cabinet of the Czech Republic") and [chair of the Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chair_of_the_Chamber_of_Deputies_of_the_Parliament_of_the_Czech_Republic "Chair of the Chamber of Deputies of the Parliament of the Czech Republic") to a municipality which applies for it.

of other municipalities. Statutory cities are allowed to subdivide into self-governing city boroughs (sg. **městský obvod**) or city parts (sg. **městská část**) with their own elected councils; such a statutory city has to issue a statute (**statut**) that delimits power to boroughs. However, only seven statutory cities have done so. Cities [Brno](/wiki/Brno "Brno"), [Plzeň](/wiki/Plze%C5%88 "Plzeň"), [Ústí nad Labem](/wiki/%C3%9Ast%C3%AD_nad_Labem "Ústí nad Labem") and [Pardubice](/wiki/Pardubice "Pardubice") are divided into city boroughs, and [Liberec](/wiki/Liberec "Liberec") has only one city borough with rest of the city being administered directly. [Brno](/wiki/Brno "Brno") is divided into city parts, and [Opava](/wiki/Opava "Opava") has eight city parts with rest of the city being administered directly. Also the capital of [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"), while not being *de jure* statutory city, is subdivided into similar self-governing boroughs.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Type of municipality in the Czech Republic

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Prague_07-2016_view_from_Lesser_Town_Tower_of_Charles_Bridge_img3.jpg/250px-Prague_07-2016_view_from_Lesser_Town_Tower_of_Charles_Bridge_img3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prague_07-2016_view_from_Lesser_Town_Tower_of_Charles_Bridge_img3.jpg)

Prague, capital of the Czech Republic

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Brno_-_Cathedral_of_Saints_Peter_and_Paul_III.jpg/250px-Brno_-_Cathedral_of_Saints_Peter_and_Paul_III.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brno_-_Cathedral_of_Saints_Peter_and_Paul_III.jpg)

Brno

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Ostrava%2C_pohled_z_Nov%C3%A9_radnice_2.jpg/250px-Ostrava%2C_pohled_z_Nov%C3%A9_radnice_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ostrava,_pohled_z_Nov%C3%A9_radnice_2.jpg)

Ostrava

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Plzen_with_St_Bartholomew_Cathedral_1.JPG/250px-Plzen_with_St_Bartholomew_Cathedral_1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Plzen_with_St_Bartholomew_Cathedral_1.JPG)

Plzeň

In the Czech Republic, a **statutory city** ([Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"): *statutární město*) is a [municipal corporation](/wiki/Municipal_corporation "Municipal corporation") that has been granted [city status](/wiki/City_status "City status") by [Act of Parliament](/wiki/Act_of_Parliament "Act of Parliament"). It is more prestigious than the simple title **město** ("[town](/wiki/Town "Town")"), which can be awarded by the [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_Czech_Republic "Cabinet of the Czech Republic") and [chair of the Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chair_of_the_Chamber_of_Deputies_of_the_Parliament_of_the_Czech_Republic "Chair of the Chamber of Deputies of the Parliament of the Czech Republic") to a municipality which applies for it.

## Differences of statutory city

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statutory_city_(Czech_Republic)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Differences of statutory city")]

Statutory city status is partially ceremonial; the [mayor](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor") is called **primátor**, rather than the **starosta** of other municipalities. Statutory cities are allowed to subdivide into self-governing city boroughs (sg. **městský obvod**) or city parts (sg. **městská část**) with their own elected councils; such a statutory city has to issue a statute (**statut**) that delimits power to boroughs. However, only seven statutory cities have done so. Cities [Brno](/wiki/Brno "Brno"), [Plzeň](/wiki/Plze%C5%88 "Plzeň"), [Ústí nad Labem](/wiki/%C3%9Ast%C3%AD_nad_Labem "Ústí nad Labem") and [Pardubice](/wiki/Pardubice "Pardubice") are divided into city boroughs, and [Liberec](/wiki/Liberec "Liberec") has only one city borough with rest of the city being administered directly. [Brno](/wiki/Brno "Brno") is divided into city parts, and [Opava](/wiki/Opava "Opava") has eight city parts with rest of the city being administered directly. Also the capital of [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"), while not being *de jure* statutory city, is subdivided into similar self-governing boroughs.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statutory_city_(Czech_Republic)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

The model is derived from its common origin in [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary"). Until 1928, 11 cities in the [Czech lands](/wiki/Czech_lands "Czech lands") received the statutory city title: Prague, Liberec, Brno, [Jihlava](/wiki/Jihlava "Jihlava"), [Kroměříž](/wiki/Krom%C4%9B%C5%99%C3%AD%C5%BE "Kroměříž"), [Olomouc](/wiki/Olomouc "Olomouc"), [Uherské Hradiště](/wiki/Uhersk%C3%A9_Hradi%C5%A1t%C4%9B "Uherské Hradiště"), [Znojmo](/wiki/Znojmo "Znojmo"), Opava, [Frýdek](/wiki/Fr%C3%BDdek,_Fr%C3%BDdek-M%C3%ADstek "Frýdek, Frýdek-Místek"), and [Bielsko](/wiki/Bielsko "Bielsko") (which became a part of [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") in 1920). On 1 December 1928 their count was reduced to five (Prague, Liberec, Brno, Olomouc and Opava). In 1942 Plzeň became a statutory city.[[1]](#cite_note-mvcr-1)

Between 1949 and 1967, the institute of statutory cities was canceled by reform in self-government and the establishment of [regions](/wiki/Regions_of_the_Czech_Republic "Regions of the Czech Republic"). Only Prague remained a *de facto* statutory city. After 1967, several cities received similar position as Prague (Brno, Plzeň, Ostrava and Ústí nad Labem), but the statutory city title was not used.[[1]](#cite_note-mvcr-1)

The concept was renewed after the [fall of communism](/wiki/Velvet_Revolution "Velvet Revolution") by the Act on Municipalities in 1990, which established 13 statutory cities in addition to Prague, the [capital city](/wiki/Capital_city "Capital city") which is still a *de facto* statutory city.[[1]](#cite_note-mvcr-1)

Unlike Austria, before [districts of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Districts_of_the_Czech_Republic "Districts of the Czech Republic") were abolished only the three largest cities (Brno, Ostrava and Plzeň) constituted a district *(*okres*)* on their own; the others were a part (though always a capital, except [Havířov](/wiki/Hav%C3%AD%C5%99ov "Havířov")) of a district with smaller municipalities. As the prestige associated with statutory city status grew, 12 additional statutory cities were created by the Act on Municipalities in 2000[[2]](#cite_note-2) and its four later amendments.

There are only two statutory cities, Havířov and [Třinec](/wiki/T%C5%99inec "Třinec"), that are not seats of their eponymous [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_the_Czech_Republic "Districts of the Czech Republic").

## List

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statutory_city_(Czech_Republic)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: List")]

Since August 2018, there are 26 statutory cities (plus Prague), comprising all the Czech cities over 40 thousand inhabitants (and Třinec):
